construct: Moral identity under public confrontation: how a person confronted on camera over a minor norm violation positions their behavior (moral disengagement vs. responsibility acceptance), negotiates identity (threat/defense/repair/bestowal), and performs for the recording.

salience_scale.1: Marker present but incidental; does not shape the surrounding exchange.
salience_scale.2: Marker colors the passage but the exchange would read the same without it.
salience_scale.3: Marker meaningfully shapes how the exchange should be interpreted.
salience_scale.4: Marker dominates the passage; a clear analytic anchor.
salience_scale.5: Marker defines the confrontation; the analysis cannot ignore it.

affect_definitions.anger: Hot hostility toward the other over a perceived wrong; accusatory, raised, or retaliatory wording aimed at the person.
affect_definitions.contempt: Cold dismissal from assumed superiority — the other is beneath engagement. Unlike anger, there is no heat and no grievance being pressed; the other is not worth arguing with.
affect_definitions.frustration: Irritation at a blocked goal or a stalled exchange. Unlike anger, it is aimed at the impasse or the situation rather than at the other's character.
affect_definitions.anxiety: Anticipatory unease about what is coming — exposure, consequences, or escalation not yet realized.
affect_definitions.fear: Acute alarm at an immediate threat to safety. Unlike anxiety, the danger is present and specific rather than anticipated.
affect_definitions.shame: Painful self-assessment as a bad person; the self, not merely the act, is experienced as the flaw.
affect_definitions.embarrassment: Discomfort at being seen violating a norm. Unlike shame, the exposure is the sting rather than the self; it passes with the audience.
affect_definitions.sadness: Dejection, loss, or resignation; deflated rather than mobilized.
affect_definitions.amusement: Levity or laughter, whether genuine or deployed to deflect the confrontation.
affect_definitions.pride: Displayed self-approval — satisfaction in one's own standing, conduct, or handling of the encounter.
affect_definitions.relief: Tension released once a threat, uncertainty, or confrontation resolves.
affect_definitions.neutral: No affect evident in the language; the default when wording alone does not support an affect attribution — not a claim that the speaker felt nothing.

markers.name: Moral justification
markers.definition: The act is reframed as serving a worthy or moral purpose.
indicators: recasting the violation as helpful or considerate

markers.name: Euphemistic labeling / minimization
markers.definition: Minimizing or sanitizing language reduces the act's moral weight.

markers.name: Advantageous comparison
markers.definition: The act is excused by contrast with worse conduct by others.
indicators: 'what about those fifty carts over there'
pointing to other violators or worse offenses

markers.name: Displacement of responsibility
markers.definition: Responsibility is shifted to an authority, role, or impersonal circumstance beyond the speaker's agency. Boundary: here the condition is offered to EXCUSE THE ACT; the same claim offered to DEFEND THE SELF as a good person is identity_defense. One utterance may warrant both codes.
indicators: incapacity claims offered as excusing conditions ('I have a bad back, I can't walk it over')

markers.name: Diffusion of responsibility
markers.definition: Responsibility is dissolved into a group norm.

markers.name: Distortion of consequences
markers.definition: The act's harm is denied, minimized, or questioned.

markers.name: Dehumanization
markers.definition: The confronter or victim class is stripped of moral regard — denied standing as a person whose interests count.
indicators: derogation denying the other's standing as a person worth moral consideration
'you're not even a person to me'
addressing the other as an object, animal, or category rather than as a person

markers.name: Attribution of blame
markers.definition: The confronter or another person is recast as the true wrongdoer; condemning the condemner. Boundary: blame here lands on a PERSON; responsibility shifted onto an impersonal circumstance, role, or authority is displacement_of_responsibility.
indicators: reframing the confrontation itself as the offense

markers.name: Responsibility acceptance
markers.definition: Acknowledgment of wrongdoing: admission of the act TOGETHER WITH acceptance of its wrongness — apology, self-blame, or repair. A defiant admission of the act that rejects the norm's force ('Yes, I did. It doesn't matter.') is NOT acceptance.

markers.name: Aggression / threat
markers.definition: Verbal aggression or threat of harm toward the confronter.
indicators: profanity-laden aggression directed at the confronter

markers.name: Identity threat
markers.definition: A speaker impugns the other's moral identity — attacking, discrediting, or imputing bad character. Boundary: identity_threat ATTACKS an identity the other holds; identity_bestowal CONFERS an identity the other may accept or decline. A negative label that is conferred rather than hurled may satisfy both — code both.
indicators: imputations of bad faith or bad character

markers.name: Identity defense
markers.definition: A speaker asserts a positive self-claim under threat. Boundary: here the claim DEFENDS THE SELF as a good person; the same claim offered to EXCUSE THE ACT is displacement_of_responsibility. One utterance may warrant both codes.
indicators: 'I'm trying to be as nice as I can be'
disability/exemption self-claims deployed to protect the self

markers.name: Identity repair
markers.definition: A speaker re-narrates the self after admission — restoring moral identity through ownership.
indicators: 'I screwed up and you got me'
declining mitigation to affirm the norm

markers.name: Identity bestowal
markers.definition: One speaker grants the other an identity, positive or negative — conferring a status or label the other may accept or decline. Boundary: bestowal CONFERS an identity; identity_threat ATTACKS one the other already holds. A conferred negative label may satisfy both — code both.
indicators: handing over a 'litterbug' magnet as labeling act

markers.name: Audience address / persona work
markers.definition: Speech aimed at the recording's future audience or delivered in persona.

markers.name: Camera awareness
markers.definition: A target registers being filmed or being future content.
indicators: remarks about the camera or being recorded

notes: turning_point: a turn id from the transcript, or one of arc_schema.turning_point_special_values ('off-camera' when the arc turned before recording began, null when there is no turn)
escalation phases are evidenced at flag level by aggression_threat flags falling within the episode's span

rules: marker_types: non-empty list of marker ids from this codebook
marker_types is a list: when an utterance satisfies more than one marker definition, code all that apply rather than forcing a single choice
speaker_role: canonical label of the quoted speaker; must be in coding_scope
affect: required iff any marker has requires_affect; must be from affect_vocabulary ('neutral' is the no-signal value); see affect_definitions for term glosses
quote: verbatim substring of the final transcript, single speaker only
attribution_uncertain: required true when the quoted turn's concordance < 1.0 or label is UNCLEAR
episode_id: assigned by the tool from episodes.json at validate time
t_start/t_end: seconds, within media duration, t_start <= t_end
visual_evidence: one of corroborates|contradicts|neutral, plus a short note — set only after frames are read

bystander episodes are segmentable but their turns are not coded for the defense/identity families; OTHER-labeled turns are never coded at all, since the speaker is unattributable and speaker_role must fall within coding_scope
